summary: Across 60 scored eval runs (24 as-is, 24 enhanced, 12 new-capability), the system produces a bimodal distribution: 42% score 5 (fully working) but 35% score 1-2 (broken/non-functional), yielding an overall mean of 3.37. access.js is emitted 100% of the time across all sets, with isOwner and ViewerTag as the dominant patterns (75-100% and 75-92% adoption respectively). The primary reliability problem is not prompt comprehension but generation integrity -- 7 of 12 score-1 results are catastrophically truncated App.jsx files (under 90 lines, missing export default), and another 8 low scores stem from stub/placeholder components that accept props but render static text. When generation completes intact, the model demonstrates strong access control design and feature implementation.
topFindings: access.js emitted in 100% of all 60 runs across all three sets, with well-structured validation logic even when App.jsx is broken. isOwner is used in 75-100% of runs per set, ViewerTag in 75-92%. The model has fully internalized the access control contract -- the access.js file is never the failure point.

When generation completes intact, quality is high: 25 of 60 runs scored 5/5 with fully working features, correct hook usage, coherent UI themes, and proper AI integration via callAI. Standout examples include soccer-signup-new r0 (coach/parent role separation with AI load balancing), maintenance-request-new (per-tenant channel isolation with AI triage), bookmarks-enh (three-tier role model with curator gating via hasRole), and emoji-chef-enh (voting, AI roasting, and owner-only pinning).

New-capability prompts (Set C) correctly adopt allowAnonymous at 58% -- every prompt that semantically requires anonymous access (contact-form, survey, job-application) emits it. This is a strong signal that the model reads prompt intent and maps it to the right access.js primitive, even for a feature (allowAnonymous) that never appears in Set A or B.
bottomFindings: Catastrophic truncation is the single largest failure mode: 7 of 12 score-1 results have App.jsx files under 90 lines with no export default, no imports, and no main component -- just a single surviving sub-component fragment.

Stub/placeholder components are the second failure mode (8 occurrences across scores 1-2): a component is defined but renders only static text like 'Loading...' or 'Composer lands here'. The stub always corresponds to the primary user-facing input feature.

Triple-run variance is extreme: 4 of 7 triple-run prompts show a range of 4 (scores spanning 1 to 5), meaning the same prompt can produce either a perfect app or a completely broken one. focus-timer-asis is consistently bad (1, 1, 3) -- its multi-timer + phase-switching + stats architecture may exceed a reliable single-generation complexity budget.
